frontmatter.title: The intrinsic opcleaving of a family of univalent reflexive graphs

mainmatter.p: Given a type A and a family of univalent reflexive graphs B \colon A \to \mathsf {URG}, we can always define a opcleaving rendering B fibrant over the intrinsic univalent reflexive graph on A. The opcleaving is obtained directly from the transport operation of the identification type.

frontmatter.title: Dependent univalent reflexive graphs

mainmatter.p: We have seen how to obtain principles for equational reasoning by imposing univalent reflexive graph structures on types; in general, we will need an analogous tool for working with families of types B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} given a univalent reflexive graph A. Such a tool must account for heterogeneous identifications in B, i.e. identifications between elements of different fibers of B; in this section, we explore three possible notion of dependent univalent reflexive graph:

ol.li: displayed univalent reflexive graphs provide an unbiased and heterogeneous account of identifications between elements of different fibers of B;
opfibered families of univalent reflexive graphs reduce heterogeneous identification to homogeneous identification by means of a forward transport operation;
fibered families of univalent reflexive graphs reduce heterogeneous identification to homogeneous identification by means of a backward transport operation.

mainmatter.p: All of these notions are useful: because it is unbiased (i.e. prefers neither forward nor backward transport), the displayed representation is the most fundamental; on the other hand, the biased fibered and opfibered representations are the most frequently used. The fact that the latter are described directly for families of univalent reflexive graphs in the existing sense is practically important for the compositionality of reasoning in univalent foundations, as we will more frequently have general-purpose building blocks for composing univalent reflexive graphs than for composing displayed univalent reflexive graphs.

frontmatter.title: Displayed univalent reflexive graph

mainmatter.p: Let A be a univalent reflexive graph. A displayed reflexive graph over A is given by families of types B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} and \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _B^ \bullet \right ) \mathclose {} \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y:A \right \} \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y:A \right \} \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y:A \right \} \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left \{ x,y:A \right \} \mathclose {} } } x \cong _A y \to Bx \to By \to \mathsf {Type} together with an assignment of displayed reflexivity data 1_{ u } \colon b \cong _B^{ 1_{ x } } b for every u:Bx. The displayed reflexive graph is called univalent when for each x:A the induced reflexive graph \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _{Bx} \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( \cong ^{ 1_{ x } }_B \right ) \mathclose {} on Bx is univalent.

We will write \mathsf {DURG} \, A for the (large) type of displayed univalent reflexive graphs over A. We will at times silently coerce a displayed univalent reflexive graph B \colon \mathsf {DURG} \, A to its underlying family of vertices B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type}.

frontmatter.title: Opfibrant family of univalent reflexive graphs

mainmatter.p: Let A be a univalent reflexive graph; an A-indexed family B \colon A \to \mathsf {URG} of univalent reflexive graphs is called opfibrant when it comes equipped with an opcleaving as defined below:

ol.li: a pushforward operation sending each p \colon x \cong _A y to some specified map p_* \colon Bx \to By;

a family \Phi _B \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {} _*b \cong _{Bx} b witnessing the unit law that pushforward along the identity element 1_{ x } \colon x \cong _A x is the identity function.

frontmatter.title: Fibrant family of univalent reflexive graphs

mainmatter.p: Let A be a univalent reflexive graph; an A-indexed family B \colon A \to \mathsf {URG} of univalent reflexive graphs is called fibrant when it comes equipped with a cleaving as defined below:

ol.li: a restriction operation sending each p \colon x \cong _A y to some specified map p^* \colon By \to Bx;

a family \Phi _B \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } } b \cong _{Bx} 1_{ x } ^*b witnessing the unit law that restriction along the identity element 1_{ x } \colon x \cong _A x is the identity function.

mainmatter.p: Our goal is to show that fibrant and opfibrant families of univalent reflexive graphs both give rise to appropriate displayed univalent reflexive graphs on their base. We will do this incrementally.

frontmatter.title: Display of opfibrant families of univalent reflexive graphs

mainmatter.p: Let A be a univalent reflexive graph. Any opfibrant family of univalent reflexive graphs B \colon A \to \mathsf {URG} gives rise to a canonical displayed univalent reflexive graph over A in which:

ol.li: for p \colon x \cong _A y, we define b \cong _B^p b' to be p_*b \cong _{By} b';

for x:A and b:Bx, the identity element 1_{ b } \colon b \cong _B^{ 1_{ x } }b is given by the component \Phi _Bxb \colon \mathopen {} \left ( 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {} _*b \cong _{Bx}b of the unit law of B.

mainmatter.block: Proof. Univalence is easily established using the univalence of each Bx

frontmatter.title: The total univalent reflexive graph of a displayed univalent reflexive graph

mainmatter.p: Let A be a univalent reflexive graph structure, and let B: \mathsf {DURG} \, A be a displayed univalent reflexive graph structure over A. The total univalent reflexive graph of B over A, written A.B, is defined as follows:

ol.li: vertices of A.B are taken in the dependent sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx};

edges are defined by \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{A.B} \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} : \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \alpha :x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \alpha :x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \alpha :x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( \alpha :x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } } u \cong _B^{ \alpha }v;
reflexivity data is given by assigning 1_{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} } : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( 1_{ x } , 1_{ u } \right ) \mathclose {}.

mainmatter.p: Univalence is established in two singleton inductions, first using univalence of A followed by univalence of B.

frontmatter.title: Covariant sum of an opfibrant family of univalent reflexive graphs

mainmatter.p: Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {URG} be an opfibrant family of univalent reflexive graphs over a univalent reflexive graph A. We define the covariant sum of B \colon A \to \mathsf {URG}, written \mathchoice { \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, to be the total univalent reflexive graph of the displayed univalent reflexive graph associated to B. In particular, we have defined \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \oint _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}} \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} to be \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } } p_*u \cong _{By} v.

frontmatter.title: The univalence principle for reflexive graphs

mainmatter.p: We may define a univalent reflexive graph structure on the type of reflexive graphs with vertices in a given type A, so that an isomorphism from \mathopen {} \left ( G, 1_{ \bullet } \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( G', 1_{ \bullet } ' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a pair of a pointwise equivalence of types H \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Gxx \cong G'xx that preserves reflexivity data as witnessed by a homotopy I \colon \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} H \, x \, x \, 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {} \sim 1_{ \bullet } '.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: On the type of edges A \to A \to \mathsf {Type} we may impose the place the canonical univalent reflexive graph structure given by the dependent product \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x,y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathsf {Type} where \mathsf {Type} is equipped with its standard univalent reflexive graph structure: in particular, an equivalence of graphs G \cong _{A \to A \to \mathsf {Type} } G' is given by a pointwise family of equivalences of types.

On a given reflexivity datum 1_{ \bullet } \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Gxx over G \colon A \to A \to \mathsf {Type}, we impose the univalent reflexive graph structure given by dependent homotopies.

Next, we argue that the family of univalent reflexive graphs given by homotopies of reflexivity data over graphs is opfibrant. Given an equivalence H : G \cong G' of graphs and a reflexivity datum 1_{ \bullet } \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Gxx, we define H_* 1_{ \bullet } x : \equiv H \, x \, x \, 1_{ x }; the unit law holds definitionally.
Therefore, we may equip the type of reflexive graphs with the covariant sum of the family of univalent reflexive graphs given by homotopies of reflexivity data over graph equivalences.

frontmatter.title: Display of fibrant families of univalent reflexive graphs

mainmatter.p: Let A be a univalent reflexive graph. Any fibrant family of univalent reflexive graphs B \colon A \to \mathsf {URG} gives rise to a canonical displayed univalent reflexive graph over A in which:

ol.li: for p \colon x \cong _A y, we define b \cong _B^p b' to be b \cong _{By} p^*b';
for x:A and b:Bx, the identity element 1_{ b } \colon b \cong _B^{ 1_{ x } }b is given by the component \Phi _Bxb \colon b \cong _{Bx} 1_{ x } ^*b of the unit law of B.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Univalence is more difficult to establish than for the case of opfibrant families. Fixing a:A, we must show that the reflexive graph structure on B a with edges \mathopen {} \left ( b \cong _B^{ 1_{ a } } b' \right ) \mathclose {} \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( b \cong _{Ba} 1_{ a } ^*b \right ) \mathclose {} and reflexivity elements for b:Ba given by \Phi _B ab \colon b \cong _{Ba} 1_{ a } ^*b is univalent.

We proceed by identifying this reflexive graph structure with the assumed one on Ba using the univalence principle for reflexive graphs, and using the fact that the latter is assumed to be univalent. The desired equivalence of reflexive graphs is given by straightforward path algebra.

frontmatter.title: Contravariant sum of fibrant families of univalent reflexive graphs

mainmatter.p: Let B \colon A \to \mathsf {URG} be an fibrant family of univalent reflexive graphs over a univalent reflexive graph A. We define the contravariant sum of B \colon A \to \mathsf {URG}, written \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}, to be the total univalent reflexive graph of the displayed univalent reflexive graph associated to B. In particular, we have defined \mathopen {} \left ( x,u \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } Bx} \mathopen {} \left ( y,v \right ) \mathclose {} to be \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : x \cong _A y \right ) \mathclose {} } } u \cong _{Bx} p^*v.

frontmatter.title: The univalence principle for fibers

mainmatter.p: Given f \colon A \to B and b:B, we shall impose a univalent reflexive graph structure on the fiber \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } fx=b of f over b. The family \mathopen {} \left ( f-=b \right ) \mathclose {} \colon A \to \mathsf {URG} given by intrinsic univalent reflexive graphs in each component is fibrant:

ol.li: for p \colon x = y and \phi :fy=b, we define p^*b \colon fx=b to be identification \mathsf {ap}_{ f } p \cdot \phi;
the restriction counit law can be seen to hold using the unit laws for composition of identification.

mainmatter.p: Therefore, we may compute the contravariant sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {fx=b} using the cleaving above, with \mathopen {} \left ( x, \phi \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathchoice { \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \int _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {fx=b}} \mathopen {} \left ( y, \psi \right ) \mathclose {} definitionally equal to \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p \colon x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p \colon x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p \colon x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p \colon x= y \right ) \mathclose {} } } \phi = \mathsf {ap}_{ f } p \cdot \psi.

frontmatter.title: The standard cleaving for sectionhood witnesses

mainmatter.p: Let f \colon A \to B be a given function. Given a homotopy H \colon s \sim s' \colon B \to A and a witness h \colon f \circ s' \sim 1_{ B } that s' is a section of f, we may construct a witness H^*h \colon f \circ s \sim 1_{ B } that s is a section of f as follows:
mainmatter.tex: H^*h x : \equiv \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \mathopen {} \left ( Hx \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h x
mainmatter.p: As we evidently have 1_{ s } ^*h x = \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \mathsf {refl} \cdot h x = h x, the data above constitute a cleaving of f \circ- \sim 1_{ B } over B \to A, both with their standard univalent reflexive graph structure as given by (dependent) homotopies. In this sense, sectionhood witnesses constitute a fibrant family of univalent reflexive graphs.

frontmatter.title: The standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

mainmatter.p: Let f \colon A \to B be a given function. Given a homotopy H \colon r \sim r' \colon B \to A and a witness h \colon r' \circ f \sim 1_{ A } that r' is a retraction of f, we may construct a witness H^*h \colon r \circ f \sim 1_{ A } that r is a retraction of f as follows:
mainmatter.tex: H^*h x : \equiv H \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h x
mainmatter.p: As we have 1_{ r } ^*h x = h x, the data above constitute a cleaving of - \circ f \sim 1_{ B } over B \to A, both with their standard univalent reflexive graph structure as given by (dependent) homotopies. Thus retractionhood witnesses constitute a fibrant family of univalent reflexive graphs.

frontmatter.title: The univalence principle for sections

mainmatter.p: A suitable univalent reflexive graph on sections to a fixed function r \colon A \to B is given by a contravariant sum of univalent reflexive graphs, as sectionhood witnesses are fibrant over their underlying sections. In particular, an isomorphism of sections of r from \mathopen {} \left ( s,h \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( s',h' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a homotopy H_s \colon s \sim s' together with a higher homotopy H_h \colon h \sim \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} \mathsf {ap}_{ r } \mathopen {} \left ( H_s x \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h'x \right ) \mathclose {}.

frontmatter.title: The univalence principle for retractions

mainmatter.p: A suitable univalent reflexive graph on retractions to a fixed function s \colon B \to A is given by a contravariant sum of univalent reflexive graphs, as retractionhood witnesses are fibrant over their underlying sections. In particular, an isomorphism of retractions of s from \mathopen {} \left ( r,h \right ) \mathclose {} to \mathopen {} \left ( r',h' \right ) \mathclose {} is given by a homotopy H_r \colon r \sim r' together with a higher homotopy H_h \colon h \sim \mathopen {} \left ( \lambda x \mathpunct {.} H_r \mathopen {} \left ( r x \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot h'x \right ) \mathclose {}.

frontmatter.title: Opfibrant family of univalent reflexive graphs

mainmatter.p: Let A be a univalent reflexive graph; an A-indexed family B \colon A \to \mathsf {URG} of univalent reflexive graphs is called opfibrant when it comes equipped with an opcleaving as defined below:

ol.li: a pushforward operation sending each p \colon x \cong _A y to some specified map p_* \colon Bx \to By;

a family \Phi _B \colon \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A,b:Bx \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {} _*b \cong _{Bx} b witnessing the unit law that pushforward along the identity element 1_{ x } \colon x \cong _A x is the identity function.

frontmatter.title: The intrinsic univalent reflexive graph

mainmatter.p: The intrinsic univalent reflexive graph on a type A is defined by setting x \cong _A y : \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( x = y \right ) \mathclose {} and 1_{ x } : \equiv \mathsf {refl}.
mainmatter.block: Proof. That the reflexive graph defined above is univalent follows immediately by identification induction.

frontmatter.title: Univalent reflexive graph

mainmatter.p: A reflexive graph is given by a type A together with a family of types \mathopen {} \left ( \cong _A \right ) \mathclose {} \colon A \to A \to \mathsf {Type} and an assignment 1_{ x } : x \cong x to each x,y: A. The reflexive graph is called univalent when for all x:A the type \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } x \cong _A y is a proposition, and so is contractible with centre \mathopen {} \left ( x, 1_{ x } \right ) \mathclose {}.
We will write \mathsf {URG} for the (large) type of univalent reflexive graphs. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given univalent reflexive graph to its underlying type of vertices.

frontmatter.title: Dependent sums of propositions

mainmatter.p: If A is a proposition and B \colon A \to \mathsf {Type} is a proposition, then the dependent sum \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} is a proposition.
mainmatter.block: Proof. We fix pairs \mathopen {} \left ( a,b \right ) \mathclose {} and \mathopen {} \left ( a',b' \right ) \mathclose {} in \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx}. By means of the total univalent reflexive graph on the pointwise displayed univalent reflexive graph on B, it suffices to exhibit identifications p \colon a = a' and p_* b = b'. These are given respectively by our assumptions that A and Ba' are propositions.
